content.text: Azure Cloud Shell is a stateless container: it starts fresh on every session. To persist scripts, configuration, and output between sessions, Cloud Shell mounts a dedicated Azure file share into the container. This mounted share is the only storage that survives when a Cloud Shell session ends.

Understanding how this storage works — what is persistent, what is ephemeral, how capacity and costs are managed, and how to share or back up the data — is essential for using Cloud Shell effectively.

When you first launch Cloud Shell, Azure automatically provisions a storage account in your chosen region if one does not already exist. Inside that account, a file share is created and mounted into the Cloud Shell container via SMB 3.0.
Survives session end; 5 GB default quota
pip, npm, go install lost on restart
Bash history is preserved; PowerShell history is not by default

# Inspect the mounted file share
df -h ~/clouddrive
# Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
# //cs-..../...   5.0G  120M  4.9G   3% /home/user/clouddrive

# Confirm ~ is a symlink to clouddrive
ls -la ~
# lrwxrwxrwx 1 user user ... /home/user -> /home/user/clouddrive/user

# See the storage account and share that Cloud Shell uses
az cloudshell show-storage 2>/dev/null || true
# Alternatively look for accounts tagged with ms-resource-usage=azure-cloud-shell
az storage account list \
  --query "[?tags.\"ms-resource-usage\"=='azure-cloud-shell'].{name:name, rg:resourceGroup, loc:location}" \
  -o table
First-Time Setup: Choosing or Providing Storage

The first time you open Cloud Shell you are prompted to create a new storage account or to use an existing one. You can accept the defaults (auto-create in a new resource group) or click 'Show advanced settings' to provide your own.
Any subscription you have access to
Any existing or new resource group
Must be globally unique, 3–24 chars, lowercase alphanumeric
Any valid file share name (3–63 chars)
After setup, Cloud Shell remembers the storage account for subsequent sessions in that region. You do not need to re-configure storage on every launch.

# List the Cloud Shell resource group if auto-created
az group list \
  --query "[?starts_with(name, 'cloud-shell-storage')].name" \
  -o tsv

# Show the file share used by Cloud Shell
ACCOUNT=$(az storage account list \
  --query "[?tags.\"ms-resource-usage\"=='azure-cloud-shell'].name | [0]" \
  -o tsv)
az storage share list --account-name "$ACCOUNT" -o table

You can use an existing storage account instead of letting Azure create one. This is useful when you want to keep Cloud Shell storage inside a governed resource group with your own RBAC, tags, and lifecycle policies.
~/clouddrive is your primary persistent workspace. Use it to store scripts, configuration files, output data, and any artifacts you want to keep between sessions.

# Create a project directory
mkdir -p ~/clouddrive/projects/myapp
cd ~/clouddrive/projects/myapp

# Write a script
cat > deploy.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group "${RG}" \
  --template-file main.bicep
EOF
chmod +x deploy.sh

# Check directory size
du -sh ~/clouddrive/*

# Clean up old files to free space
find ~/clouddrive -name '*.log' -mtime +30 -delete

# PowerShell: same clouddrive, object-oriented
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path ~/clouddrive/projects/myapp -Force
Set-Location ~/clouddrive/projects/myapp

# Write a PowerShell script
@'
param([string]$ResourceGroup)
New-AzResourceGroupDeployment `
  -ResourceGroupName $ResourceGroup `
  -TemplateFile main.bicep
'@ | Set-Content deploy.ps1

# Check directory sizes
Get-ChildItem ~/clouddrive | ForEach-Object {
    $size = (Get-ChildItem $_.FullName -Recurse -File | Measure-Object -Property Length -Sum).Sum
    [PSCustomObject]@{ Name = $_.Name; SizeMB = [math]::Round($size/1MB, 2) }
} | Sort-Object SizeMB -Descending

The Cloud Shell toolbar provides an Upload/Download button for transferring small files to and from your local machine. For larger or automated transfers, use AzCopy or the Azure CLI storage commands.

# Upload a file: use the toolbar Upload button
# File lands in ~/  (home directory / clouddrive)

# Download a file to your local machine:
download ~/clouddrive/report.csv
# This opens a browser download prompt for the file

# For automated transfers, use azcopy (pre-installed)
ACCOUNT=mystorageaccount
SHARE=myshare
SAS=$(az storage share generate-sas \
  --account-name $ACCOUNT \
  --name $SHARE \
  --permissions rwdl \
  --expiry $(date -u -d '+1 hour' '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ') \
  -o tsv)

azcopy copy '/local/path/data.csv' \
  "https://${ACCOUNT}.file.core.windows.net/${SHARE}/data.csv?${SAS}"

# PowerShell: download a file
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri 'https://example.com/data.json' -OutFile ~/clouddrive/data.json

# Upload from clouddrive to Azure Blob Storage
$ctx = New-AzStorageContext -StorageAccountName 'mystorageaccount' -UseConnectedAccount
Set-AzStorageBlobContent `
  -Container 'uploads' `
  -File ~/clouddrive/report.csv `
  -Blob 'reports/report.csv' `
  -Context $ctx

# Download from Azure Blob Storage to clouddrive
Get-AzStorageBlobContent `
  -Container 'data' `
  -Blob 'input.json' `
  -Destination ~/clouddrive/input.json `
  -Context $ctx
The default Cloud Shell file share has a 5 GB quota. When the quota is reached, writes fail and Cloud Shell may behave unexpectedly. Monitor usage and clean up regularly.

# Check current usage
df -h ~/clouddrive

# Find largest files/directories
du -sh ~/clouddrive/* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -20

# Find files larger than 100 MB
find ~/clouddrive -size +100M -printf '%s\t%p\n' | sort -rn

# Archive old logs (compress instead of delete)
find ~/clouddrive/logs -name '*.log' -mtime +7 | xargs tar czf ~/clouddrive/logs_archive.tar.gz --remove-files

# Increase the file share quota (requires Storage Account access)
ACCOUNT=$(az storage account list \
  --query "[?tags.\"ms-resource-usage\"=='azure-cloud-shell'].name | [0]" -o tsv)
SHARE=$(az storage share list --account-name $ACCOUNT --query '[0].name' -o tsv)

az storage share update \
  --account-name $ACCOUNT \
  --name $SHARE \
  --quota 50   # increase to 50 GB (Standard tier supports up to 100 TB)

# PowerShell: check usage
[math]::Round((Get-ChildItem ~/clouddrive -Recurse -File | Measure-Object -Property Length -Sum).Sum / 1GB, 2)

# Find largest files
Get-ChildItem ~/clouddrive -Recurse -File |
  Sort-Object Length -Descending |
  Select-Object -First 20 FullName, @{N='SizeMB'; E={[math]::Round($_.Length/1MB,2)}}

# Increase file share quota
$account = (Get-AzStorageAccount | Where-Object { $_.Tags['ms-resource-usage'] -eq 'azure-cloud-shell' })[0]
$ctx = $account.Context
$share = (Get-AzStorageShare -Context $ctx)[0]
Set-AzStorageShareQuota -Share $share -Quota 50   # increase to 50 GB
Increase file share quota to 50 GB
Use lifecycle management to delete old blobs
Reduces quota usage, no cost impact
Accessing Cloud Shell Storage from Outside Cloud Shell
Because ~/clouddrive is a standard Azure Files share, you can mount it from other machines, access it via REST, or manipulate it with AzCopy — all without being inside Cloud Shell.

# Mount the Cloud Shell file share on Windows
$storageAccountName = 'mystorageaccount'
$sharePassword = (Get-AzStorageAccountKey -ResourceGroupName myRG -AccountName $storageAccountName)[0].Value
$securePass = ConvertTo-SecureString $sharePassword -AsPlainText -Force
$cred = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential ('Azure\' + $storageAccountName, $securePass)

New-PSDrive -Name Z -PSProvider FileSystem `
  -Root "\\${storageAccountName}.file.core.windows.net\cs-myuser-hash" `
  -Credential $cred -Persist

# Or use net use from cmd.exe:
# net use Z: \\mystorageaccount.file.core.windows.net\cs-myuser-hash /user:Azure\mystorageaccount <key>

# Mount the Cloud Shell file share on Linux (requires cifs-utils)
sudo apt-get install -y cifs-utils    # Debian/Ubuntu

STORAGE_ACCOUNT=mystorageaccount
SHARE_NAME=cs-myuser-hash
STORAGE_KEY=$(az storage account keys list --account-name $STORAGE_ACCOUNT --query '[0].value' -o tsv)

sudo mkdir -p /mnt/cloudshell
sudo mount -t cifs \
  //${STORAGE_ACCOUNT}.file.core.windows.net/${SHARE_NAME} \
  /mnt/cloudshell \
  -o username=${STORAGE_ACCOUNT},password=${STORAGE_KEY},serverino,nosharesock,actimeo=30

ls /mnt/cloudshell    # your Cloud Shell files

# macOS: same but use:
# sudo mount -t smbfs //Azure\\${STORAGE_ACCOUNT}:${STORAGE_KEY}@${STORAGE_ACCOUNT}.file.core.windows.net/${SHARE_NAME} /mnt/cloudshell

# Sync local directory to Cloud Shell storage (bidirectional)
azcopy sync /local/scripts/ \
  "https://mystorageaccount.file.core.windows.net/cs-myuser-hash/scripts/" \
  --recursive

# Copy output files from Cloud Shell storage to a blob container
azcopy copy \
  "https://mystorageaccount.file.core.windows.net/cs-myuser-hash/outputs/" \
  "https://myblobaccount.blob.core.windows.net/backups/" \
  --recursive

Although Cloud Shell storage is redundant within Azure (LRS or ZRS depending on region), it is not automatically backed up. Accidental deletion of files in ~/clouddrive is permanent without a backup strategy.

# Strategy 1: Sync to a separate blob container daily (add to a scheduled runbook)
ACCOUNT=mystorageaccount
SHARE=cs-myuser-hash
BACKUP_CONTAINER=cloudshell-backups

az storage container create --account-name $ACCOUNT --name $BACKUP_CONTAINER 2>/dev/null || true

# Use azcopy to copy all files from the file share to blob container
SAS=$(az storage account generate-sas \
  --account-name $ACCOUNT \
  --permissions 'rwdl' \
  --resource-types sco \
  --services bf \
  --expiry $(date -u -d '+1 day' '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ') \
  -o tsv)

azcopy copy \
  "https://${ACCOUNT}.file.core.windows.net/${SHARE}?${SAS}" \
  "https://${ACCOUNT}.blob.core.windows.net/${BACKUP_CONTAINER}?${SAS}" \
  --recursive

# Strategy 2: Push scripts to a Git repository
cd ~/clouddrive/scripts
git init && git add -A && git commit -m 'backup $(date +%Y-%m-%d)'
git push origin main

# Strategy 3: Azure Backup for Azure Files (portal or CLI)
# Create a Recovery Services vault
az backup vault create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name csBackupVault \
  --location eastus

# Enable backup for the Cloud Shell file share
az backup protection enable-for-azurefileshare \
  --vault-name csBackupVault \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --policy-name AzureFileSharePolicy \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount \
  --azure-file-share cs-myuser-hash

# Trigger an on-demand backup
az backup protection backup-now \
  --vault-name csBackupVault \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --item-name cs-myuser-hash \
  --container-name 'StorageContainer;storage;myRG;mystorageaccount' \
  --retain-until $(date -u -d '+30 days' '+%d-%m-%Y')
Resetting or Changing Cloud Shell Storage
You may need to reset Cloud Shell storage if the file share becomes corrupted, you want to switch to a different storage account, or you are setting up Cloud Shell for a new Azure account.
In the Azure portal, open Cloud Shell.
Click the gear icon (Settings) in the Cloud Shell toolbar.
Select 'Reset user settings'. This unmounts the current file share but does not delete the storage account or its data.
On the next Cloud Shell launch, the storage configuration prompt appears again.
Choose 'Show advanced settings' to specify a different storage account and file share.

# After a reset: verify the new storage mount
df -h ~/clouddrive

# Re-attach to the OLD file share's data manually if needed
# (files are still in the Azure Files share, even after reset)
# Mount the old share inside Cloud Shell:
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/oldshare
OLD_ACCOUNT=oldstorageaccount
OLD_SHARE=cs-myuser-oldhash
OLD_KEY=$(az storage account keys list --account-name $OLD_ACCOUNT --query '[0].value' -o tsv)
sudo mount -t cifs \
  //${OLD_ACCOUNT}.file.core.windows.net/${OLD_SHARE} \
  /mnt/oldshare \
  -o username=${OLD_ACCOUNT},password=${OLD_KEY}

# Copy files from old share to new clouddrive
cp -r /mnt/oldshare/scripts ~/clouddrive/
sudo umount /mnt/oldshare

Resetting Cloud Shell settings does not delete your data. The Azure Files share and its contents remain in your storage account. You can re-attach the same share or a different one on the next session.
Cloud Shell storage costs are charged to your Azure subscription based on the storage consumed in the file share. Understanding the pricing tiers helps you avoid unexpected bills.

If you are not using Cloud Shell regularly, the storage account still exists and incurs a small ongoing cost. To eliminate the cost entirely, delete the storage account and resource group — but this also deletes all your Cloud Shell scripts and configuration.

# Find the Cloud Shell storage account resource group
RG=$(az group list \
  --query "[?starts_with(name, 'cloud-shell-storage')].name | [0]" \
  -o tsv)

# Delete the resource group (and all Cloud Shell storage) — IRREVERSIBLE
# az group delete --name $RG --yes --no-wait

# Alternatively, just stop the file share transactions by removing the mount config
# (Reset settings in Cloud Shell; the storage persists but is not mounted)
Because ~/clouddrive persists between sessions, you can store shell configuration files there and have them automatically loaded every time a new Cloud Shell session starts.

# ~/.bashrc is in ~/clouddrive and loads automatically on every Bash session
# Add persistent configurations:
cat >> ~/.bashrc << 'BASHEOF'
# Azure CLI defaults
export AZURE_DEFAULTS_GROUP="production-rg"
export AZURE_DEFAULTS_LOCATION="eastus"

# Convenience aliases
alias k='kubectl'
alias azls='az resource list -g $AZURE_DEFAULTS_GROUP -o table'
alias deploys='az deployment group list -g $AZURE_DEFAULTS_GROUP -o table'

# Auto-install tools not pre-installed
if ! command -v yq &>/dev/null; then
  pip install --user yq --quiet
fi
BASHEOF
source ~/.bashrc

# Store Kubernetes config (persists between sessions)
az aks get-credentials \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myCluster \
  --file ~/.kube/config
# ~/.kube/ is inside ~/clouddrive, so the credentials persist

# $PROFILE points to ~/clouddrive/.config/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1
# It loads automatically on every PowerShell Cloud Shell session

if (!(Test-Path (Split-Path $PROFILE))) {
    New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path (Split-Path $PROFILE) -Force
}

# Add to $PROFILE
Add-Content $PROFILE @'
# Set default resource group
Set-AzDefault -ResourceGroupName 'production-rg'

# Import custom module from clouddrive
Import-Module ~/clouddrive/modules/AzureHelpers.psm1 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

# Useful functions
function Get-AllVMs {
    Get-AzVM -Status | Select-Object Name, ResourceGroupName, PowerState
}
Set-Alias vms Get-AllVMs
'@

. $PROFILE   # apply immediately
Sharing Cloud Shell Storage Across Users
A single Azure Files share can be mounted by multiple users, enabling team script sharing, shared tooling, or read-only reference libraries accessible to everyone in a team.

# Grant another user access to your Cloud Shell storage account
ACCOUNT=mystorageaccount
USER_OBJECT_ID=$(az ad user show --id colleague@example.com --query id -o tsv)

# Assign Storage File Data SMB Share Reader role (read-only)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $USER_OBJECT_ID \
  --role 'Storage File Data SMB Share Reader' \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/${ACCOUNT}"

# Or assign Storage File Data SMB Share Contributor for read-write access
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $USER_OBJECT_ID \
  --role 'Storage File Data SMB Share Contributor' \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/${ACCOUNT}"

# The colleague then configures their Cloud Shell to use the same storage account
# via 'Show advanced settings' when first launching Cloud Shell
Storage File Data SMB Share Reader
Storage File Data SMB Share Contributor
Storage File Data SMB Share Elevated Contributor
DevOps teams managing Cloud Shell infrastructure

Use separate file shares (not separate directories in one share) to isolate user data when multiple users share the same storage account. Each Cloud Shell user should have their own file share to avoid conflicts.
Common Cloud Shell storage issues and how to resolve them.
az storage share update --quota 50 to increase quota
Move files to ~/clouddrive before ending session
Cloud Shell fails to mount storage
Storage account deleted or permissions changed
Reset Cloud Shell settings and re-configure storage
SMB port 445 blocked by network policy
Use a network that allows outbound port 445, or use a VNet-integrated Cloud Shell
Ensure .bashrc and $PROFILE are in ~/clouddrive (they should be by default)
Large file upload fails via toolbar
Toolbar upload is limited to ~50 MB files
Use azcopy or az storage file upload for large files

# Debug: verify the file share is properly mounted
mount | grep clouddrive
# should show: //account.file.core.windows.net/share on /home/user/clouddrive type cifs

# Test write access
echo 'test' > ~/clouddrive/write_test.txt && echo 'Write OK' || echo 'Write FAILED'
rm -f ~/clouddrive/write_test.txt

# Check storage account network rules (if blocked)
ACCOUNT=$(az storage account list \
  --query "[?tags.\"ms-resource-usage\"=='azure-cloud-shell'].name | [0]" -o tsv)
az storage account show --name $ACCOUNT \
  --query networkRuleSet -o json

# If network rules are blocking, allow Azure services
az storage account update --name $ACCOUNT \
  --bypass AzureServices \
  --default-action Allow

Cloud Shell Storage is an Azure Files share automatically provisioned and mounted into your Cloud Shell container. Files in ~/clouddrive persist across sessions; everything else (installed packages, /tmp, background processes) is ephemeral. The default quota is 5 GB — monitor usage, archive old files, and increase the quota through the Azure portal or CLI when needed. You can access the file share from outside Cloud Shell by mounting it via SMB or using AzCopy for bulk transfers. For critical scripts, supplement Azure Files persistence with Git version control or Azure Backup snapshots.
